metadata.title: Why a Particle in a Box Cannot Sit Still
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = quantum / wave / allowed; CRIMSON = classical / zero / forbidden
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no eight-step boundary-value derivation, no normalization constant calculation, no n² spectrum table. One mechanism only: wavefunction must vanish at both walls so lowest mode is a half-wave; that curvature IS momentum and momentum IS kinetic energy so E1 > 0.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A classical marble in a box can sit on the floor — perfectly still, zero energy. The quantum particle in the same box can't. Its lowest energy is stubbornly above zero.

card.copy: Why a Particle in a Box Cannot Sit Still

beats.narration_text: Drop a marble in a box — it rolls to the bottom and stops. Energy: zero. That's classical mechanics and it makes complete sense.
Now the quantum version. There's a box. A particle inside. Classical prediction: the lowest energy should be zero — just sit still. It doesn't. Why?

card.copy: Classical physics predicts E = 0 is allowed. The quantum particle's lowest energy is above zero. Why?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Now the quantum version. There's a box. A particle inside. Classical prediction: the lowest energy should be zero — just sit still. It doesn't. Why?

beats.narration_text: The wavefunction describes where the particle might be found. At both walls, the particle simply cannot exist — so the wavefunction must be exactly zero there.
Zero at both ends. The wavefunction starts at zero on the left wall, rises somewhere in the middle, and must return to zero at the right wall.
The simplest curve that does this is a half-wavelength — one arch of a sine wave. That's the ground state. It is the minimum possible shape.
Now here is the key: a curved wavefunction means the particle has momentum. A flatter curve means less — but the curve cannot go flat. It still has to arch from zero to zero.
Momentum means kinetic energy. The tighter the box, the sharper the minimum arch, the more curvature, the higher the ground state energy. Confinement forces the energy up.
What if the wavefunction were flat — a horizontal line at zero? That would mean zero energy. But a flat line through zero is no particle at all — the probability of finding it anywhere would be zero.
Illustrative example: a 1-nanometer box. The ground state energy works out to about 0.38 electron-volts — visible light carries around 2 eV. That gap is real and measurable.
The ground state cannot vanish — not because of any extra assumption, but because the boundary conditions demand a curve, and any curve carries energy. The zero is unreachable.

card.copy: The wavefunction must arch. Any arch is momentum. Momentum is energy. Zero is forbidden.
